109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}

117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
122	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
123	\end{theorem}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
128	\begin{proof}

130	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels

134	
135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
157	
158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.

171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. When the helper's Lean proof relies on a fact with no blueprint entry, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" (or create it too, if it belongs in your chapter).

200	
201	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
202	
203	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
204	
205	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
206	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
207	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
208	
209	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
210	
211	```latex
212	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
213	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
214	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
215	  \mathlibok
216	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
217	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
218	\end{lemma}
219	```
220	
221	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
222	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
223	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
224	
225	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
28	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
30	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
33	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\), i.e.\ \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~REF below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its \(\text{Ext}\) reformulation
47	    (Definition~REF and Lemma~REF),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~REF),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (Lemma~REF),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (Theorem~REF), and
54	  \item the corollary regular \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (Corollary~REF).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (Section~REF) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes Corollary~REF to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.
60	
61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65	
66	\leanok
67	  [Depth of a module]
68	  \label{def:depth}
69	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth}
70	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth)
71	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17748-L17759).
72	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
73	  % "Let \(R\) be a ring, and \(I \subset R\) an ideal. Let \(M\) be a finite \(R\)-module.
74	  %  The {\it \(I\)-depth} of \(M\), denoted \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is defined as follows:
75	  %  \begin{enumerate}
76	  %  \item if \(IM \not = M\), then \(\text{depth}_I(M)\) is the supremum in
77	  %  \(\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences in \(I\),
78	  %  \item if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\).
79	  %  \end{enumerate}
80	  %  If \((R, \mathfrak m)\) is local we call \(\text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M)\) simply
81	  %  the {\it depth} of \(M\)."
82	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth).}
83	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(I \subseteq R\) be an ideal, and let \(M\) be a finitely
84	  generated \(R\)-module. The \emph{\(I\)-depth} of \(M\), written \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is the
85	  supremum in \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences contained
86	  in \(I\), provided \(IM \neq M\); if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\). When
87	  \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is a Noetherian local ring we abbreviate
88	  \(\text{depth}(M) := \text{depth}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)\) and call this simply the
89	  \emph{depth} of \(M\). The depth of the ring \(R\) itself is \(\text{depth}(R)\), the depth
90	  of \(R\) as a module over itself.
91	\end{definition}
92	
93	Recall that a sequence \(f_1, \dots, f_r \in R\) is called \emph{\(M\)-regular} if
94	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0\) and for each \(i\), the element \(f_i\) is a non-zero-divisor on
95	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M\). The empty sequence is regular on \(M\) iff \(M \neq 0\) (it is
96	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention \(\text{depth}(0) = \infty\)
97	above is the usual one).
98	
99	The two practical computations of depth are the regular-sequence definition (used to
100	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the \(\text{Ext}\) characterisation (used to establish an
101	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).
102	
103	\begin{lemma}
104	
105	\leanok
106	  [Depth via \(\text{Ext}\)]
107	  \label{lem:depth_via_ext}
108	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index}
109	  
110	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext)
111	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17845-L17885).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
113	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\).
114	  %  Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module. Then \(\text{depth}(M)\)
115	  %  is equal to the smallest integer \(i\) such that
116	  %  \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\) is nonzero."
117	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext).}
118	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring with residue field
119	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak{m}\) and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely generated \(R\)-module. Then
120	  \[
121	    \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \inf\bigl\{\, i \in \mathbb{N} \;\bigm|\; \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \,\bigr\}.
122	  \]
123	\end{lemma}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
126	% "Let \(\delta(M)\) denote the depth of \(M\) and let \(i(M)\) denote
127	%  the smallest integer \(i\) such that \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\)
128	%  is nonzero. We will see in a moment that \(i(M) < \infty\).
129	%  By Lemma REF we have
130	%  \(\delta(M) = 0\) if and only if \(i(M) = 0\), because
131	%  \(\mathfrak m \in \text{Ass}(M)\) exactly means
132	%  that \(i(M) = 0\). Hence if \(\delta(M)\) or \(i(M)\) is \(> 0\), then we may
133	%  choose \(x \in \mathfrak m\) such that (a) \(x\) is a nonzerodivisor
134	%  on \(M\), and (b) \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta(M) - 1\).
135	%  Consider the long exact sequence
136	%  of Ext-groups associated to the short exact sequence
137	%  \(0 \to M \to M \to M/xM \to 0\) by Lemma REF:
138	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
139	%  Since \(x \in \mathfrak m\) all the maps $\Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)
140	%  \to \Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)$ are zero, see
141	%  Lemma REF.
142	%  Thus it is clear that \(i(M/xM) = i(M) - 1\). Induction on
143	%  \(\delta(M)\) finishes the proof."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	  
148	  
149	  
150	\leanok
151	  Write \(\delta := \text{depth}(M)\) and \(i := \inf\{ j : \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \}\).
152	  The base case \(\delta = 0\) is exactly \(\text{Hom}_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0\) (some element of
153	  \(M\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\), i.e.\ \(\mathfrak{m} \in \text{Ass}(M)\), i.e.\
154	  \(\mathfrak{m}\) contains a zero-divisor on \(M\)), which is \(i = 0\). For the inductive step
155	  pick \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) a non-zero-divisor on \(M\) with
156	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta - 1\). The short exact sequence
157	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{x} M \to M/xM \to 0\) induces a long exact sequence of $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa,
158	  -)\(. Multiplication by \)x\( annihilates each \)\text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ (because
159	  \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) and \(\kappa\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\)), so the long exact
160	  sequence breaks into short pieces giving
161	  \(\text{Ext}^{j-1}_R(\kappa, M/xM) \cong \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)\) at the relevant
162	  indices, and the smallest non-vanishing index drops by \(1\). By induction
163	  \(i(M/xM) = i - 1 = \delta - 1\), so \(i = \delta\).
164	\end{proof}

166	\section{Projective dimension}
167	\label{sec:ab_pd}
168	
169	\begin{definition}
170	
171	\leanok
172	  [Projective dimension]
173	  \label{def:projective_dimension}
174	  \lean{Module.projectiveDimension}
175	  % NOTE: Mathlib pinned at b80f227 may already expose this as
176	  % `Module.projectiveDimension` via
177	  % `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.ProjectiveDimension` /
178	  % `Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ProjectiveResolution`; the prover should re-export
179	  % rather than re-define if the API is present. This block fixes the
180	  % mathematical content the prover must match.
181	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(M\) be an \(R\)-module. The \emph{projective
182	  dimension} of \(M\), written \(\text{pd}_R(M)\), is the infimum in
183	  \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths \(n\) of projective resolutions
184	  \[
185	    0 \longrightarrow P_n \longrightarrow P_{n-1} \longrightarrow \cdots
186	    \longrightarrow P_1 \longrightarrow P_0 \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
187	  \]
188	  with each \(P_i\) a projective \(R\)-module. We say \(M\) has \emph{finite projective dimension}
189	  if \(\text{pd}_R(M) < \infty\), and \emph{infinite projective dimension} otherwise. By
190	  convention \(\text{pd}_R(0) = -\infty\) (some references); we use \(\text{pd}_R(0) = 0\) here
191	  (the empty resolution \(0 \to 0\) of length \(0\)), but the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula in
192	  Theorem~REF concerns only nonzero \(M\).
193	  In the local Noetherian setting a finitely generated \(R\)-module of finite projective
194	  dimension admits a \emph{minimal finite free resolution}: a resolution with each \(P_i\)
195	  free of finite rank and each transition map \(P_{i+1} \to P_i\) landing in \(\mathfrak{m} P_i\).
196	\end{definition}
197	
198	The two basic facts about \(\text{pd}_R(M)\) needed downstream are:
199	\begin{itemize}
200	  \item \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) iff \(M\) is projective (in the local Noetherian setting,
201	    iff \(M\) is free);
202	  \item if \(0 \to K \to P \to M \to 0\) is a short exact sequence with \(P\) projective, then
203	    \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_R(K) + 1\) provided \(K \neq 0\).
204	\end{itemize}
205	Both are standard homological algebra and are recorded under
206	\texttt{Module.projectiveDimension\_succ\_iff} or similar in Mathlib.
207	
208	\section{Depth on a short exact sequence}
209	\label{sec:ab_depth_ses}
210	
211	\begin{lemma}
212	
213	\leanok
214	  [Depth on a short exact sequence]
215	  \label{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
216	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_of_short_exact}
217	  
218	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses)
219	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17887-L17920).
220	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
221	  % "Let \(R\) be a local Noetherian ring. Let \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\)
222	  %  be a short exact sequence of nonzero finite \(R\)-modules.
223	  %  \begin{enumerate}
224	  %  \item
225	  %  \(\text{depth}(N) \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N'), \text{depth}(N'')\}\)
226	  %  \item
227	  %  \(\text{depth}(N'') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N') - 1\}\)
228	  %  \item
229	  %  \(\text{depth}(N') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N'') + 1\}\)
230	  %  \end{enumerate}"
231	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses).}
232	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let
233	  \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of nonzero finitely generated
234	  \(R\)-modules. Then
235	  \begin{enumerate}
236	    \item \(\text{depth}(N) \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N'),\; \text{depth}(N'')\bigr)\);
237	    \item \(\text{depth}(N'') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N') - 1\bigr)\);
238	    \item \(\text{depth}(N') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N'') + 1\bigr)\).
239	  \end{enumerate}
240	\end{lemma}
241	
242	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
243	% "Use the characterization of depth using the Ext groups
244	%  \(\Ext^i(\kappa, N)\), see Lemma REF,
245	%  and use the long exact cohomology sequence
246	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
247	%  from Lemma REF."
248	\begin{proof}
249	  
250	  
251	  
252	  
253	  
254	\leanok
255	  By \cref{lem:depth_via_ext}, \(\text{depth}(N')\) (resp.\ \(\text{depth}(N)\),
256	  \(\text{depth}(N'')\)) is the smallest index at which \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\) is
257	  nonzero on \(N'\) (resp.\ \(N\), \(N''\)). Apply the long exact \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\)
258	  sequence to \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\):
259	  \[
260	    \cdots \to \text{Ext}^{i-1}_R(\kappa, N'')
261	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N')
262	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N)
263	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N'')
264	    \to \text{Ext}^{i+1}_R(\kappa, N') \to \cdots
265	  \]
266	  Each of the three inequalities is then a direct read-off of the fact that if two
267	  of the three modules in a length-3 chunk vanish then the middle vanishes (for (1)) /
268	  the boundary terms vanish (for (2),(3)). Compare with the diagonal vanishing argument in
269	  the proof of Lemma~REF.
270	\end{proof}

272	\section{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula}
273	\label{sec:ab_main}
274	
275	\begin{theorem}
276	
277	\leanok
278	  [Auslander--Buchsbaum formula]
279	  \label{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}
280	  \lean{RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula}
281	  
282	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum)
283	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L27207-L27280).
284	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
285	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring. Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module
286	  %  which has finite projective dimension \(\text{pd}_R(M)\). Then we have
287	  %  \(\)
288	  %  \text{depth}(R) = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)
289	  %  \(\)"
290	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum); cf.\
291	  Matsumura, \emph{Commutative Ring Theory}, Theorem 19.1; Auslander--Buchsbaum,
292	  \emph{Homological dimension in local rings}, 1957.}
293	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely
294	  generated \(R\)-module of finite projective dimension. Then
295	  \[
296	    \text{pd}_R(M) \;+\; \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \text{depth}(R).
297	  \]
298	\end{theorem}
299	
300	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
301	% "We prove this by induction on \(\text{depth}(M)\). The most interesting
302	%  case is the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\). In this case, let
303	%  \(\)
304	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
305	%  \(\)
306	%  be a minimal finite free resolution, so \(e = \text{pd}_R(M)\).
307	%  By Lemma REF we may assume all matrix
308	%  coefficients of the maps in the complex are contained in the maximal
309	%  ideal of \(R\). Then on the one hand, by
310	%  Proposition REF we see that
311	%  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, breaking the long
312	%  exact sequence into short exact sequences
313	%  \begin{align*}
314	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e - 1}} \to K_{e - 2} \to 0,\\
315	%  0 \to K_{e - 2} \to R^{n_{e - 2}} \to K_{e - 3} \to 0,\\
316	%  \ldots,\\
317	%  0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
318	%  \end{align*}
319	%  we see, using Lemma REF, that
320	%  \begin{align*}
321	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1,\\
322	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2,\\
323	%  \ldots,\\
324	%  \text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e - 1),\\
325	%  \text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e
326	%  \end{align*}
327	%  and since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we conclude \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\).
328	%  This finishes the proof of the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).
329	%
330	%  Induction step. If \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), then we pick \(x \in \mathfrak m\)
331	%  which is a nonzerodivisor on both \(M\) and \(R\). This is possible, because
332	%  either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\) and \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the aforementioned
333	%  Proposition REF or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which
334	%  case \(M\) is finite free hence also \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\).
335	%  Thus \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by Lemma REF
336	%  (for example) and we can find an \(x \in \mathfrak m\) which is a nonzerodivisor
337	%  on both \(R\) and \(M\). Let
338	%  \(\)
339	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
340	%  \(\)
341	%  be a minimal resolution as above. An application of the snake lemma
342	%  shows that
343	%  \(\)
344	%  0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to
345	%  M/xM \to 0
346	%  \(\)
347	%  is a minimal resolution too. Thus \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\).
348	%  By Lemma REF we have
349	%  \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
350	%  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\).

352	%  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) and similarly for \(R/xR\).
353	%  By induction hypothesis we see that the
354	%  Auslander-Buchsbaum formula holds for \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\). Since the
355	%  depths of both \(R/xR\) and \(M/xM\) have decreased by one and the projective
356	%  dimension has not changed we conclude."
357	\begin{proof}
358	  
359	  
360	  
361	\leanok
362	  We induct on \(\text{depth}(M)\).
363	
364	  \emph{Base case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).} Let \(e := \text{pd}_R(M)\) and pick a minimal
365	  finite free resolution
366	  \[
367	    0 \longrightarrow R^{n_e} \longrightarrow R^{n_{e-1}} \longrightarrow
368	    \cdots \longrightarrow R^{n_0} \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
369	  \]
370	  with all transition maps having matrix coefficients in \(\mathfrak{m}\) (this is the
371	  ``minimal-resolution-after-trimming-trivial-summands'' normalisation, cf.\ Stacks
372	  \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}). On the one hand, the ``what is exact'' criterion
373	  (Stacks tag 00MF, \texttt{proposition-what-exact}, expressing exactness of a sequence of
374	  finite free modules in terms of depths of ideals of \(r\)-minors) gives
375	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, splitting the resolution into short exact
376	  sequences
377	  \[
378	    0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to K_{e-2} \to 0, \quad
379	    0 \to K_{e-2} \to R^{n_{e-2}} \to K_{e-3} \to 0, \quad \dots, \quad
380	    0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0,
381	  \]
382	  and applying \cref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence} iteratively,
383	  yields the chain of inequalities
384	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1\),
385	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2\), \dots,
386	  \(\text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e-1)\), and finally
387	  \(\text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e\). Since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we deduce
388	  \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\). Combined with the previous inequality, $\text{depth}(R) = e =
389	  \text{pd}_R(M) + 0 = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)$.
390	
391	  \emph{Inductive step \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\).} We claim there exists \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\)
392	  which is a non-zero-divisor on both \(R\) and \(M\). Indeed: either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\), in
393	  which case \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the ``what is exact'' criterion applied to the start
394	  of a minimal resolution, or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which case \(M\) is finite free and
395	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\). Either way both \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) and
396	  \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), hence \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by
397	  \cref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}, so a common non-zero-divisor $x \in
398	  \mathfrak{m}\( exists. Take a minimal finite free resolution of \)M$ as above; the snake
399	  lemma applied to multiplication by \(x\) gives a minimal finite free resolution
400	  \[
401	    0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \cdots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to M/xM \to 0
402	  \]
403	  of \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\), of the same length \(e\). So
404	  \(\text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)\). By Stacks
405	  \texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}, both \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
406	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\), and one verifies
407	  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) (regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}\)
408	  correspond to regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}/(x)\)). Apply the inductive hypothesis to
409	  \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\):
410	  \[
411	    \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) + \text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(R/xR).
412	  \]
413	  Substituting yields
414	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + (\text{depth}(M) - 1) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\), i.e.\
415	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)\), as required.
416	\end{proof}

503	\subsection{Inductive-step substrate: per-gap decomposition of
504	  \texorpdfstring{\(\text{pd}(M) = k+1\)}{pd(M) = k+1}}
505	\label{subsec:succ_pd_gap_sequence}
506	
507	\paragraph{Status: RESOLVED iter-202.}
508	\cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd} and the main formula
509	\cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} are both closed axiom-clean as of
510	iter-202, by a \(\texttt{Nat}\)-induction restructuring
511	(\(\texttt{induction}\,k\,\texttt{generalizing}\,M\)): the inductive
512	step uses the kernel SES with
513	\(\texttt{depth\_of\_short\_exact}\) parts (2),(3) directly (no
514	exactness criterion), and the base case \(\texttt{pd}\,M = 1\) uses
515	the iter-201 matrix-collapse helper
516	(\(\texttt{ext\_comp\_mk}_0\texttt{\_ofHom\_eq\_zero\_of\_entries\_mem\_annihilator}\))
517	together with the long exact sequence. \textbf{Gap (2) (Stacks 00MF,
518	the ``what is exact'' criterion) was OBVIATED} by this Path~B route ---
519	no Buchsbaum--Eisenbud exactness criterion was needed. \textbf{All
520	gaps (1)--(4) are CLOSED or OBVIATED.} The decomposition below is
521	retained as the historical dependency analysis that motivated the
522	substrate builds; it is no longer an open work-list.
523	
524	\medskip
525	The inductive step
526	\cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}
527	of the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula was originally gated, against an
528	earlier pinned Mathlib snapshot, by three absent substrate ingredients
529	(gaps (1)--(3)) plus a fourth in hand
530	(gap (4), \cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}). This subsection records the
531	decomposition that the proof block of
532	\cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd} folded into four bullets:
533	its purpose was to make the dependency graph and the per-gap effort
534	estimates explicit, so that the substrate gaps could be formalised
535	independently (and where the dependency graph permitted, in parallel).
536	
537	\paragraph{Sequencing constraint.} The three open gaps are
538	\emph{not} mutually independent at the substrate level. Gap (3)
539	(snake-lemma carving of the minimal resolution by an
540	\(M\)-regular element \(x \in \mathfrak m\)) needs gap (1)
541	(minimal-resolution normalisation: every finite free resolution of
542	a finite module over a Noetherian local ring trims to a minimal one
543	of the same projective dimension) as input, because the snake-lemma
544	output is only known to be a \emph{minimal} resolution of \(M/xM\)
545	over \(R/(x)\) of the same length when the input resolution is itself
546	minimal. Gap (2) (Stacks 00MF, the ``what is exact'' criterion in
547	terms of depths of \(r\)-minors of the transition matrices) is
548	independent of gaps (1) and (3) at the substrate level: it is a
549	self-contained criterion on a complex of finite free modules and
550	feeds the closure assembly without going through gap (1) or gap (3).
551

553	input to'':

604	The gap (1) estimate is anchored to Bruns--Herzog,

608	length of the resolution. The gap (2) estimate reflects the chain
609	Bruns--Herzog Theorem~1.4.13 \(\to\) Eagon--Northcott
610	\(\to\) Buchsbaum--Eisenbud's exactness criterion (Stacks 00MF /

612	complex of finite free modules in terms of depths of the ideals of
613	\(r\)-minors of the transition matrices. Gap (2) is the largest of

616	
617	\paragraph{Gap (3) OBVIATED iter-200.} The iter-200 ALIGN\_WITH\_MATHLIB
618	pivot (\ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot}) replaces the literal
619	\(\texttt{ChainComplex}\,\mathbb N\) carrier with the abstract
620	\(\texttt{HasProjectiveDimensionLT}\) SES-descent pattern; under this
621	pattern, minimality of the resolution is \emph{not} used as input
622	anywhere in the inductive step. Consequently the snake-lemma carving
623	of the minimal resolution (gap (3)) is no longer a substrate
624	requirement: only gap (2) (\(\texttt{pd}\,M > 0 \Rightarrow
625	\texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\)) remains the binding gap for
626	the closure of \ref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}, and the
627	per-syzygy step of gap (1) is consumed only through the SES at
628	\(0 \to \ker f \to R^n \to M \to 0\) (no minimality input required).
629	See \ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot} for the SES-descent details.
630	
631	\paragraph{Gap (2) Stacks 00MF proof recipe.} The
632	Buchsbaum--Eisenbud exactness criterion (Stacks 00MF /
633	\texttt{proposition-what-exact}) reads: a complex
634	\(\dots \to R^{r_2} \xrightarrow{\varphi_2} R^{r_1}
635	 \xrightarrow{\varphi_1} R^{r_0}\) of finite free modules over a
636	Noetherian local ring \(R\) is exact at \(R^{r_i}\) (for \(i \geq 1\))
637	if and only if \(\texttt{rank}\,\varphi_{i+1} + \texttt{rank}\,\varphi_i
638	= r_i\) and \(\texttt{depth}\,I(\varphi_i) \geq i\), where
639	\(I(\varphi)\) denotes the ideal of \(r\)-minors of \(\varphi\) for
640	\(r = \texttt{rank}\,\varphi\). The recipe for an iter-201+ prover:
641	(a) define \(I(\varphi) \subseteq R\) as the ideal of all
642	\(\texttt{rank}\,\varphi\)-minors of \(\varphi\) (Mathlib supports
643	this via \(\texttt{Matrix.minors}\) and \(\texttt{Ideal.span}\) over the
644	finite index set of \(\binom{n}{r}\) tuples); (b) prove the depth
645	lower bound via induction on \(i\) using a Koszul-complex argument
646	on the regular sequence implicit in the exactness data; (c) derive
647	\(\texttt{pd}\,M > 0 \Rightarrow \texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\)
648	as a corollary by taking the start \(\dots \to R^{r_1} \to R^{r_0} \to
649	M\) of a minimal resolution and applying the criterion at \(i = 1\).
650	Estimated \(\sim 150-200\) LOC project-side; Mathlib upstream PR
651	candidate.
652	
653	\paragraph{Closure assembly (iter-200 ALIGN\_WITH\_MATHLIB pivot).} The
654	iter-200 substrate landing in \ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot}
655	replaces the original (1)+(3)-dependent assembly with an SES-descent
656	recipe: given an \(M\) with \(\texttt{pd}_R(M) = k+1\), the
657	per-syzygy step of gap (1)
658	(\ref{lem:exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}) supplies a
659	surjection \(f : R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\); the SES

662	\(\texttt{hasProjectiveDimensionLT\_succ\_of\_hasProjectiveDimensionLT\_ker}\)
663	give \(\texttt{pd}_R(\ker f) = k\) exactly; gap (2) (the
664	Buchsbaum--Eisenbud criterion at the start of a minimal resolution)
665	delivers \(\texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\); the iter-200
666	helper
667	\(\texttt{depth\_ker\_ge\_min\_of\_surjection\_finite\_localRing}\)
668	gives \(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f \geq \min(\texttt{depth}\,R,
669	\texttt{depth}\,M + 1) = \texttt{depth}\,M + 1\); a complementary LES
670	upper bound (or the IH applied to \(\ker f\) under the strict
671	descent of \(\texttt{pd}\)) gives the matching upper bound. Combine

674	\(k + \texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,R\), which rearranges

676	Estimated closure-assembly cost (gaps in hand): \(\sim 50-80\) LOC
677	inductive code.

698	\subsection{Gap (1) first-step substrate (iter-199): per-syzygy
699	  minimal surjection from a finite free module}
700	\label{subsec:ab_gap1_first_step}
701	
702	\begin{lemma}
703	
704	\leanok
705	  [Per-syzygy minimal surjection from \(R^n\) for finite modules
706	    over a local ring]
707	  \label{lem:exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}
708	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}
709	  \uses{def:depth}
710	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LK
711	  % (lemma-add-trivial-complex; minimal-resolution carving substrate)
712	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L24590-L24623).
713	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
714	  % "Suppose $R$ is a ring. Let
715	  %     \ldots
716	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i + 1}}
717	  %     R^{n_i}
718	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_i}
719	  %     R^{n_{i-1}}
720	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i-1}}
721	  %     \ldots
722	  %  be a complex of finite free $R$-modules. Suppose that for some $i$
723	  %  some matrix coefficient of the map $\varphi_i$ is invertible.
724	  %  Then the displayed complex is isomorphic to the direct sum of a
725	  %  complex
726	  %     \ldots \to
727	  %     R^{n_{i + 2}} \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i + 2}}
728	  %     R^{n_{i + 1}} \to
729	  %     R^{n_i - 1} \to
730	  %     R^{n_{i - 1} - 1} \to
731	  %     R^{n_{i - 2}} \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i - 2}}
732	  %     R^{n_{i - 3}} \to
733	  %     \ldots
734	  %  and the complex $\ldots \to 0 \to R \to R \to 0 \to \ldots$ where
735	  %  the map $R \to R$ is the identity map."
736	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LK
737	    (\texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}).}
738	  Let \(R\) be a local ring (Noetherian-ness NOT required at this
739	  step), \(\mathfrak m \subseteq R\) the maximal ideal,
740	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\) the residue field, and \(M\) a finite
741	  \(R\)-module. Then there exist \(n \in \mathbb N\) and an
742	  \(R\)-linear surjection \(f : R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\) such that:
743	  \begin{enumerate}
744	    \item \(n = \dim_\kappa(\kappa \otimes_R M)\) (the minimal
745	      generator count of \(M\), via Nakayama);
746	    \item \(\ker f \subseteq \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\) (the
747	      ``minimality'' condition: every entry of every relation lives
748	      in the maximal ideal, so the chosen lift has no trivial
749	      summands).
750	  \end{enumerate}
751	\end{lemma}
752	\begin{proof}
753	
754	\leanok
755	  Pick a \(\kappa\)-basis
756	  \(b : \mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \kappa \otimes_R M\) via
757	  \texttt{Module.finBasis} with
758	  \(n = \dim_\kappa(\kappa \otimes_R M)\). Surjectivity of the
759	  canonical map \(M \twoheadrightarrow \kappa \otimes_R M\)
760	  (composition of \texttt{TensorProduct.mk\_surjective} and
761	  \texttt{Ideal.Quotient.mk\_surjective}) gives lifts
762	  \(m_i \in M\) with \(1 \otimes m_i = b_i\). Define
763	  \(f : R^n \to M\) by \(f x := \sum_i x_i \cdot m_i\) via
764	  \texttt{(Pi.basisFun R (Fin n)).constr R m}.
765	
766	  Surjectivity: \(\mathrm{range}\, f = \mathrm{span}_R(m_i)\) by
767	  \texttt{Module.Basis.constr\_range}, and
768	  \(\mathrm{span}_R(m_i) = M\) by Nakayama via
769	  \texttt{IsLocalRing.span\_eq\_top\_of\_tmul\_eq\_basis}.
770	
771	  Kernel containment: for \(x \in \ker f\) the relation
772	  \(\sum_i x_i m_i = 0\) tensors to
773	  \(\sum_i (1 \otimes x_i \cdot m_i) = 0\); using
774	  \texttt{TensorProduct.tmul\_smul} and the linear independence of
775	  \(b\) (\texttt{Module.Basis.linearIndependent}) we obtain
776	  \(\mathrm{residue}_R(x_i) = 0\) for every \(i\), i.e.\
777	  \(x_i \in \mathfrak m\). Decomposing
778	  \(x = \sum_i x_i \cdot \mathrm{Pi.single}\,i\,1\) then yields
779	  \(x \in \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\) by
780	  \texttt{Submodule.smul\_mem\_smul} and
781	  \texttt{Submodule.sum\_mem}.
782	\end{proof}
783	
784	This lemma is the \emph{per-syzygy} step of gap (1)'s
785	minimal-resolution carving: given a finite \(R\)-module \(M\)
786	over a local ring \(R\), it produces a minimal surjection
787	\(R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\). Iter-200's ALIGN\_WITH\_MATHLIB pivot
788	(\ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot}) replaces the original
789	\(\mathbb N\)-recursive \(\texttt{ChainComplex}\) assembly target
790	with abstract \(\texttt{HasProjectiveDimensionLT}\) SES descent: only
791	the per-syzygy SES is consumed; the iterated chain-complex carrier
792	is not needed.

906	\paragraph{Iter-201 Path B closure recipe (LES-injectivity +
907	matrix-collapse).} The iter-201 \texttt{ab-stacks00mf} mathlib-
908	analogist verdict identifies an alternative closure that sidesteps
909	gap (2): instead of building Stacks 00MF as a standalone substrate,
910	split \ref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd} into an
911	\emph{inductive step} (\(\texttt{pd}\,M = k+1, k \geq 1\)) and a
912	\emph{base case} (\(\texttt{pd}\,M = 1\)). Each piece uses only
913	already-existing LES infrastructure plus one new
914	\(\sim 50\)--\(80\) LOC matrix-collapse helper.
915	
916	\textit{Inductive step \(\texttt{pd}\,M = k+1, k \geq 1\)
917	\((\sim 50\)--\(80\) LOC).} From
918	\ref{lem:hasProjectiveDimensionLT_ker_of_surjection} +
919	the contrapositive of
920	\ref{lem:hasProjectiveDimensionLT_succ_of_hasProjectiveDimensionLT_ker},
921	\(\texttt{pd}\,\ker f = k\) exactly. Strong induction gives
922	\(k + \texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,R\), hence
923	\(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f < \texttt{depth}\,R\) since \(k \geq 1\).
924	Lower bound \(\texttt{depth}\,M \geq \texttt{depth}\,\ker f - 1\):
925	\(\texttt{depth\_of\_short\_exact}\) part (2). Upper bound
926	\(\texttt{depth}\,M \leq \texttt{depth}\,\ker f - 1\): by
927	contradiction --- assume \(\texttt{depth}\,M \geq \texttt{depth}\,\ker f\),
928	look at the LES of \(\texttt{Ext}^*(\kappa, -)\) at
929	\(i = \texttt{depth}\,\ker f\), infer
930	\(\texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,\ker f}(\kappa, R^n) \neq 0\)
931	(forcing \(\texttt{depth}\,R \leq \texttt{depth}\,\ker f\)),
932	contradicting the IH.
933	
934	\textit{Base case \(\texttt{pd}\,M = 1\) \((\sim 80\)--\(120\) LOC).}
935	\(\ker f\) is projective (\(\texttt{pd}\,\ker f = 0\)), hence free
936	over the local Noetherian \(R\) by Mathlib's
937	\(\texttt{Module.free\_of\_flat\_of\_isLocalRing}\) +
938	\(\texttt{Module.Flat.of\_projective}\). So \(\ker f \cong R^m\) with
939	\(m \geq 1\). The inclusion \(\ker f \hookrightarrow R^n\) is an
940	\((n \times m)\)-matrix \(A\) with entries in
941	\(\texttt{Ann}(\kappa) = \mathfrak m\) (from
942	\ref{lem:exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}'s minimality
943	clause \(\ker f \subseteq \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\)).
944	
945	The \textbf{matrix-collapse helper}
946	(CLOSED iter-201, 4 axiom-clean private decls at
947	\texttt{AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} L1418--L1517):
948	\[
949	  \forall p \geq 0,\quad \forall e \in \texttt{Ext}^p(\kappa, R^n),\quad
950	  e \circ \texttt{Ext.mk}_0\,(\texttt{ofHom}\,A) = 0
951	  \quad \text{in } \texttt{Ext}^{p+0}(\kappa, R^n)
952	  \text{ when } A_{ij} \in \texttt{Ann}_R\,\kappa.
953	\]
954	The 4 helpers are \(\texttt{Module.elemMap}\) (the elementary map
955	sending \(\texttt{Pi.single}\,j\,1 \mapsto \texttt{Pi.single}\,i\,1\)
956	and other basis vectors to zero), \(\texttt{Module.elemMap\_apply}\),
957	\(\texttt{Module.linearMap\_finFunR\_matrix\_decomp}\)
958	(\(A = \sum_{i,j} A(\texttt{Pi.single}\,j\,1)\,i \cdot
959	   \texttt{elemMap}_{ij}\)), and the closure helper
960	\(\texttt{Module.ext\_comp\_mk}_0\texttt{\_ofHom\_eq\_zero\_of\_entries\_mem\_annihilator}\)
961	itself. The realised API path uses Mathlib's
962	\(\texttt{ModuleCat.hom\_sum}\) + \(\texttt{Ext.mk}_0\texttt{\_sum}\)
963	+ \(\texttt{Ext.comp\_sum}\) and the scalar siblings
964	\(\texttt{ModuleCat.hom\_smul}\) + \(\texttt{Ext.mk}_0\texttt{\_smul}\)
965	+ \(\texttt{Ext.comp\_smul}\) (\emph{not}
966	\(\texttt{Ext.bilinearCompOfLinear}\) as originally projected; the
967	mathematical content is identical). Each summand reduces to a scalar-
968	bilinear form killed by the existing
969	\(\texttt{ext\_smul\_eq\_zero\_of\_mem\_annihilator}\) (L229).
970	
971	With matrix-collapse, the LES at \(i = \texttt{depth}\,R - 1\)
972	gives
973	\[
974	  \texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R - 1}(\kappa, M)
975	  \xrightarrow{\;\delta\;}
976	  \texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R}(\kappa, \ker f)
977	  \xrightarrow{\;(- \circ A)\;}
978	  \texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R}(\kappa, R^n)
979	\]
980	with the second arrow zero. If
981	\(\texttt{depth}\,M \geq \texttt{depth}\,R\), the source is zero,
982	hence \(\texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R}(\kappa, \ker f) = 0\) ---
983	but \(\texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R}(\kappa, \ker f) \simeq
984	\texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R}(\kappa, R^m) \neq 0\)
985	(\(m \geq 1\), \(\texttt{depth}\,R^m = \texttt{depth}\,R\)).
986	Contradiction. So \(\texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\), and the
987	base case
988	\(\texttt{pd}\,M + \texttt{depth}\,M = \texttt{depth}\,R\) follows
989	from \(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,R\) (since \(\ker f\)
990	free).
991

1032	\section{Corollary: regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay}
1033	\label{sec:ab_regular_cm}
1034	
1035	\begin{definition}
1036	
1037	\leanok
1038	  [Cohen--Macaulay local ring]
1039	  \label{def:cohen_macaulay_local}
1040	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay}
1041	  \uses{def:depth}
1042	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM)
1043	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25288-L25299).
1044	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1045	  % "A Noetherian local ring \(R\) is called {\it Cohen-Macaulay}
1046	  %  if it is Cohen-Macaulay as a module over itself."
1047	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM).}
1048	  A Noetherian local ring \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is called \emph{Cohen--Macaulay} if
1049	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\) (equivalently, if \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay as a module
1050	  over itself, where Cohen--Macaulay for a finite \(R\)-module \(M\) means
1051	  \(\dim(\text{Supp}(M)) = \text{depth}(M)\), cf.\ Stacks tag 00N3).
1052	\end{definition}
1053	
1054	% NOTE (iter-185 review, supersedes iter-184): the Lean assembly

1071	% (c) Mathlib upstream.
1072	\begin{corollary}
1073	
1074	\leanok
1075	  [Regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay]
1076	  \label{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
1077	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular}
1078	  \uses{def:cohen_macaulay_local, thm:auslander_buchsbaum}
1079	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM)
1080	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25714-L25730).
1081	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1082	  % "Let \(R\) be a regular local ring and let
1083	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) be a minimal set of generators
1084	  %  for the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\). Then
1085	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) is a regular sequence, and
1086	  %  each \(R/(x_1, \ldots, x_c)\) is a regular local ring
1087	  %  of dimension \(d - c\). In particular \(R\) is Cohen-Macaulay."
1088	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM).}
1089	  Every regular Noetherian local ring is Cohen--Macaulay: if \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is
1090	  regular local of Krull dimension \(d\), then \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\).
1091	\end{corollary}
1092	
1093	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1094	% "Note that \(R/x_1R\) is a Noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\)
1095	%  by Lemma REF with \(x_2, \ldots, x_d\)
1096	%  generating the maximal ideal. Hence it is a regular local ring by definition.
1097	%  Since \(R\) is a domain by Lemma REF
1098	%  \(x_1\) is a nonzerodivisor."
1099	\begin{proof}
1100	  
1101	  
1102	  
1103	  
1104	  
1105	\leanok
1106	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be regular of dimension \(d\) and pick a minimal generating set
1107	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) of \(\mathfrak{m}\). By Stacks tag 00NQ (lemma-regular-domain), \(R\) is
1108	  an integral domain, so \(x_1\) is a non-zero-divisor. The quotient \(R/x_1 R\) is a Noetherian
1109	  local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\) (by Krull's principal ideal theorem,
1110	  \texttt{lemma-one-equation}) with maximal ideal generated by \(x_2, \dots, x_d\), hence
1111	  has dimension exactly \(d - 1\) and is again regular of dimension \(d - 1\). Inducting on \(d\),
1112	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) is an \(R\)-regular sequence, so
1113	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq d\). The reverse inequality \(\text{depth}(R) \leq \dim(R) = d\)
1114	  always holds for any nonzero finite module over a Noetherian local ring
1115	  (Stacks tag 00LK, \texttt{lemma-bound-depth}). Thus \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\), i.e.\
1116	  \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay.
1117	
1118	  \emph{Remark on the Auslander--Buchsbaum route.} The same conclusion can be obtained from
1119	  \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} as follows: take \(M := R\) itself. Then \(M\) is
1120	  finitely generated and projective (free of rank \(1\)), so \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\). The
1121	  formula gives \(\text{depth}(R) = 0 + \text{depth}(R)\), a tautology. The non-tautological
1122	  use of Auslander--Buchsbaum is rather: for a regular local ring of dimension \(d\), every
1123	  finitely generated module \(M\) has \(\text{pd}_R(M) \leq d\) (Stacks tag 00O2,
1124	  \texttt{proposition-finite-gl-dim-regular}); applying the formula to such an \(M\) then
1125	  relates \(\text{depth}(M)\) to \(d - \text{pd}_R(M)\), which is the form most often used in
1126	  practice. The direct regular-sequence argument above is the cleanest proof of
1127	  \(R\) Cohen--Macaulay itself.
1128	\end{proof}
1129	
1130	\section{Application: codim-1 extension on a regular surface (gating A.4.a)}
1131	\label{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}
1132	
1133	The downstream consumer is A.4.a: extending a rational map
1134	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from a regular projective surface \(S\) to an abelian variety \(A\)
1135	across a closed point \(x \in S\) that is missing from the domain of definition \(U \subseteq S\).
1136	The classical mechanism (cf.\ Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, III.7) is:
1137	
1138	\begin{enumerate}
1139	  \item The local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) is a regular Noetherian local ring of Krull
1140	    dimension \(2\) (because \(S\) is regular and \(\dim S = 2\)).
1141	  \item By Corollary~REF it is Cohen--Macaulay, so
1142	    \(\text{depth}(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}) = 2\). Equivalently, the maximal ideal contains an
1143	    \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\)-regular sequence of length \(2\) (any two transverse generators).
1144	  \item Sections of the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_S\) on \(U = S \setminus \{x\}\) extend
1145	    uniquely across \(x\) because \(H^0_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) and
1146	    \(H^1_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) (these local-cohomology vanishings are equivalent to depth
1147	    \(\geq 2\) at \(x\) by Stacks tag 0AVF; see Hartshorne, III.3.5, for the surface case).
1148	  \item For a rational map \(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) with codimension-\(\geq 2\) indeterminacy
1149	    locus on a regular surface, the same depth-\(\geq 2\) input produces a sheaf-theoretic
1150	    extension of \(f\) to all of \(S\): pull back any sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_A\)-affine-locally,
1151	    obtain sections over \(U\), and extend across \(x\) by the above.
1152	\end{enumerate}
1153	
1154	So Corollary~REF is the gating input: the geometric A.4.a
1155	proof can be written conditional on the algebraic Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary, and the
1156	two halves develop independently --- as long as Theorem~REF and
1157	Corollary~REF are formalized in
1158	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} with the signatures pinned
1159	in this chapter, A.4.a can consume them as Mathlib-style imports.
1160

13	Auslander–Buchsbaum formula and the corollary "regular local ring
14	⟹ Cohen–Macaulay" that the sibling sub-build **A.4.a** (codim-1 extension of a

112	/-! ## §1. Depth of a finite module
113	
114	The `I`-depth of a finite `R`-module `M` is the supremum in
115	`{0, 1, 2, …, ∞}` of the lengths of `M`-regular sequences contained in `I`
116	(provided `IM ≠ M`; if `IM = M` we set `depth_I(M) = ∞`). Mathlib `b80f227`
117	exposes the regular-sequence predicate `RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular`
118	(`Mathlib.RingTheory.Regular.RegularSequence`) but not the resulting
119	numeric depth function — that is the gap this declaration fills.
120	
121	Blueprint reference: `def:depth` (Stacks tag 00LF). -/
122	
123	/-- The **`I`-depth** of a finite `R`-module `M`: the supremum (in `ℕ∞`) of
124	lengths of `M`-regular sequences contained in the ideal `I`.
125	
126	When `IM = M` (the "trivial-quotient" case, e.g. `M = 0` or `I = R`) the
127	supremum is taken to be `⊤` by convention. When `(R, 𝔪)` is local one usually
128	calls `depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M` simply *the depth* of `M`.
129	
130	iter-176+: the body is the supremum
131	```
132	sSup { (n : ℕ∞) | ∃ rs : List R, rs.length = n ∧ (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ I) ∧
133	                  RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular M rs }
134	```
135	folded with the `IM = M` clause. The signature is non-tautological: it
136	asserts a function `(Ideal R) → (M : Type v) → ℕ∞` matching the Stacks
137	00LF definition.
138	
139	**iter-179 Mathlib-gap check (Lane F Target 2 STRETCH)**: a pinned-commit
140	audit of `Mathlib.RingTheory.Regular.Depth` (the only file under
141	`Mathlib/RingTheory/*` containing the word "depth") confirms that Mathlib
142	`b80f227` ships only depth-zero lemmas (`IsSMulRegular.subsingleton_linearMap_iff`)
143	and *not* the numeric depth function itself. The one-liner re-export route
144	is therefore not available; the body stays a typed `sorry` until an
145	iter-180+ body lane fills the supremum-with-`IM=M` clause directly. -/
146	noncomputable def depth {R : Type u} [CommRing R] (_I : Ideal R)
147	    (_M : Type v) [AddCommGroup _M] [Module R _M] : ℕ∞ :=
148	  open Classical in
149	  if _I • (⊤ : Submodule R _M) = ⊤ then (⊤ : ℕ∞)
150	  else sSup { n : ℕ∞ | ∃ rs : List R, (rs.length : ℕ∞) = n ∧
151	    (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ _I) ∧ RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular _M rs }
152	
153	end Module
154	
155	end RingTheory

157	/-! ## §2. Projective dimension
158	
159	Mathlib `b80f227` exposes the categorical
160	`CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension : C → WithBot ℕ∞` on an abelian category
161	with enough projectives (file `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.Projective.Dimension`).
162	For `R`-modules this specialises to `ModuleCat.of R M`. The blueprint pins the
163	`Module.projectiveDimension` name as the re-export that downstream consumers
164	can use directly on an `R`-module without first packaging it in `ModuleCat`.
165	
166	Blueprint reference: `def:projective_dimension`. -/
167	
168	namespace Module
169	
170	/-- The **projective dimension** of an `R`-module `M`, defined as the
171	categorical projective dimension of `ModuleCat.of R M`.
172	
173	This is a re-export of `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension` specialised to
174	the abelian category `ModuleCat R`. The categorical definition is the
175	infimum (in `WithBot ℕ∞`) of `n : ℕ` such that all `Ext^i(M, -)` vanish for
176	`i > n`, equivalently the smallest length of a projective resolution of `M`.
177	
178	Mathlib has the categorical `projectiveDimension` and the module-specific
179	`ModuleCat.projectiveDimension_eq_of_linearEquiv`; the blueprint pins the
180	wrapper name so downstream consumers can write
181	`Module.projectiveDimension R M` rather than threading `ModuleCat.of`.
182	
183	The body is the one-line re-export
184	`CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension (ModuleCat.of R _M)`; iter-178 closed
185	this declaration kernel-clean. -/
186	noncomputable def projectiveDimension (R : Type u) [Ring R]
187	    (_M : Type u) [AddCommGroup _M] [Module R _M] : WithBot ℕ∞ :=
188	  CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension (ModuleCat.of R _M)
189	
190	end Module
191	
192	namespace RingTheory
193	
194	namespace Module

196	/-! ## §3. Depth via Ext characterisation
197	
198	For a Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)` with residue field `κ = R/𝔪` and a
199	nonzero finite `R`-module `M`, the depth of `M` equals the smallest index `i`
200	at which `Ext^i_R(κ, M)` is nonzero (Stacks tag 00LP).
201	
202	This is the lemma A.4.a's downstream consumer ultimately reads off:
203	combined with the regular-sequence definition (`depth`), the Ext
204	characterisation provides both the *lower bound* (regular sequences exhibit
205	`Ext^i = 0` for `i < length(rs)`) and the *upper bound* (failure of any
206	extension lifts a non-zero element in `Ext^{depth}(κ, M)`).
207	
208	The signature pins the equivalence via the depth-bound `↔` `Ext`-vanishing-
209	below: `n ≤ depth(M) ↔ Ext^i(κ, M) = 0 for all i < n`. This is logically
210	equivalent to "depth(M) = smallest i with Ext^i ≠ 0" and is the form most
211	convenient for inductive proofs (Stacks 00LP proof: pick `x ∈ 𝔪`
212	non-zero-divisor, use long exact `Ext^*(κ, -)` on `0 → M → M → M/xM → 0`).
213	
214	Blueprint reference: `lem:depth_via_ext` (Stacks tag 00LP). -/
215	
216	/-! ### Helper C (iter-183 Lane G, axiom-clean): `Ann`-killing of Ext via R-linearity
217	
218	For any `R`-modules `N, M` and any `x : R` in the annihilator of `N`, the
219	R-action `x • e` on `e : Ext^i_R(N, M)` is zero.
220	
221	Proof sketch: `x • e = (mk₀ (x • 𝟙_N)).comp e (zero_add i)` (by R-linearity:
222	`mk₀_smul + smul_comp + mk₀_id_comp`). For `x ∈ Ann(N)` the morphism
223	`x • 𝟙_N : N ⟶ N` is the zero map, so `mk₀ (x • 𝟙_N) = mk₀ 0 = 0`
224	(`mk₀_zero`), and `0.comp e = 0` (`zero_comp`).
225	
226	iter-183 Lane G: closed kernel-clean. This is the precise statement of the
227	Stacks-00LP "`x ∈ 𝔪` annihilates `Ext^*(κ, -)`" trick, lifted to the more
228	general `x ∈ Ann(N)` form so it covers both `N = κ` and `N = R/(x_1,…,x_k)`. -/
229	private lemma ext_smul_eq_zero_of_mem_annihilator
230	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R]
231	    {N M : ModuleCat.{u} R} {i : ℕ} (e : Abelian.Ext.{u} N M i)
232	    {x : R} (hx : x ∈ Module.annihilator R (N : Type u)) :
233	    x • e = 0 := by
234	  -- Step 1: x • 𝟙_N = 0 in ModuleCat (the underlying linear map sends m ↦ x • m,
235	  -- which is 0 since x ∈ Ann(N)).
236	  have hkill : (x • (𝟙 N : N ⟶ N)) = (0 : N ⟶ N) := by
237	    apply ModuleCat.hom_ext
238	    apply LinearMap.ext
239	    intro n
240	    change x • n = 0
241	    exact Module.mem_annihilator.mp hx n
242	  -- Step 2: x • e = (mk₀ (x • 𝟙_N)).comp e (zero_add i) by R-linearity.
243	  have hreflect :
244	      (CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.mk₀ (x • (𝟙 N : N ⟶ N))).comp e (zero_add i)
245	        = x • e := by
246	    have hmk : (CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.mk₀ (x • (𝟙 N : N ⟶ N))
247	                : Abelian.Ext.{u} N N 0)
248	        = x • CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.mk₀ (𝟙 N) :=
249	      CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.mk₀_smul (R := R) x (𝟙 N)
250	    rw [hmk, CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.smul_comp,
251	        CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.mk₀_id_comp]
252	  -- Step 3: substitute hkill to collapse mk₀ … to mk₀ 0 = 0, then zero_comp.
253	  rw [← hreflect, hkill, CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.mk₀_zero,
254	      CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.zero_comp]
255	
256	/-- **Depth via Ext characterisation.** For a Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)`
257	with residue field `κ = R/𝔪` and a nonzero finite `R`-module `M`, the
258	depth-bound `n ≤ depth(M)` is equivalent to the vanishing of `Ext^i_R(κ, M)`
259	for all `i < n`. Equivalently, `depth(M)` is the smallest `i` at which
260	`Ext^i_R(κ, M)` is nonzero.
261	
262	iter-176+: the body proceeds by induction on `n` via the long exact sequence
263	of `Ext^*(κ, -)` applied to `0 → M → M → M/xM → 0` for a non-zero-divisor
264	`x ∈ 𝔪`. The base case `n = 0` is `Hom(κ, M) ≠ 0 ↔ depth(M) = 0`, which is
265	the standard "the maximal ideal contains a zero-divisor on `M` iff
266	`𝔪 ∈ Ass(M)`" characterisation (Stacks 00LC).
267	
268	iter-183 Lane G structural progress:
269	* Generalised the inductive hypothesis over `M` (so `ih` is universally
270	  quantified in `M`, allowing recursive use on `M/xM`).
271	* Base case `n = 0` closed kernel-clean (both directions trivial).
272	* New axiom-clean helper `ext_smul_eq_zero_of_mem_annihilator` (lines
273	  229–254) discharges substantive piece (a) of the Stacks 00LP proof:
274	  *"`x ∈ Ann N` annihilates `Ext^*(N, ·)`"*, via
275	  `mk₀_smul + smul_comp + mk₀_id_comp + mk₀_zero + zero_comp`.
276	* The inductive step's **backward direction** has the regular-element
277	  extraction stage closed structurally: from the `Ext^0(κ, M) = 0`
278	  hypothesis we derive `Subsingleton (κ →ₗ[R] M)` (via `mk₀_eq_zero_iff +
279	  ModuleCat.hom_ext_iff`) and then invoke
280	  `IsSMulRegular.subsingleton_linearMap_iff` + `Ideal.annihilator_quotient`
281	  to obtain `x ∈ 𝔪` with `IsSMulRegular M x`, with
282	  `nontrivial_quotSMulTop_of_mem_maximalIdeal` providing the `Nontrivial
283	  (M/xM)` instance for the recursive IH application.
284	
285	Residual `sorry`s (2 named inline branches):
286	1. **Forward direction**  `(n+1 : ℕ∞) ≤ depth M → ∀ i ≤ n, Ext^i(κ, M) = 0` —
287	   needs: extract regular sequence of length n+1 from supremum (handle `⊤`
288	   via Nakayama); cons-decompose via `isRegular_cons_iff`; LES of Ext on
289	   `IsSMulRegular.smulShortComplex_shortExact`; recurse via `ih` on M/xM.
290	2. **Backward direction**, final assembly — after the regular-element
291	   extraction (already done), needs: LES chase to derive Ext-vanishing on
292	   M/xM at indices `j < n` (using the new helper to kill the
293	   multiplication-by-x maps); apply `ih (M := M/xM)` to obtain regular
294	   sequence `rs'`; cons `x :: rs'`; conclude via `le_sSup`. -/
295	theorem depth_eq_smallest_ext_index
296	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
297	    {M : Type u} [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] [_root_.Module.Finite R M]
298	    [Nontrivial M] (n : ℕ) :
299	    (n : ℕ∞) ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M ↔
300	      ∀ i : ℕ, i < n →
301	        ∀ e : Abelian.Ext.{u}
302	            (ModuleCat.of R (IsLocalRing.ResidueField R))
303	            (ModuleCat.of R M) i, e = 0 := by
304	  -- We generalize `M` so the inductive hypothesis is universally quantified
305	  -- over the module — this lets the induction step recursively apply the IH
306	  -- to the quotient `M / xM` (a *different* module of the same shape).
307	  induction n generalizing M with
308	  | zero =>
309	    -- LHS: `(0 : ℕ∞) ≤ depth M` is `bot_le`.
310	    -- RHS: `∀ i < 0, …` is vacuous since no `i` satisfies `i < 0`.
311	    exact ⟨fun _ i hi _ => absurd hi (Nat.not_lt_zero i), fun _ => bot_le⟩
312	  | succ n ih =>
313	    -- The Stacks 00LP inductive step. The blueprint sketch is:
314	    --
315	    -- (⇒) Assume `(n+1 : ℕ∞) ≤ depth M`. Then `Nontrivial M` rules out
316	    --     `𝔪 • ⊤ = ⊤` (Nakayama), so `depth M` is the supremum and we can
317	    --     extract an `M`-regular sequence `rs = x :: rs'` of length `n+1` in
318	    --     `𝔪`. The cons-decomposition `RingTheory.Sequence.isRegular_cons_iff`
319	    --     gives `IsSMulRegular M x` and `IsRegular (QuotSMulTop x M) rs'`.
320	    --     For `i = 0`: `Hom(κ, M) ↪ Hom(κ, M)` via `[x]` is `[x]` on the
321	    --     domain `Hom(κ, M)`, but `x ∈ 𝔪 = Ann(κ)` kills this on the κ side,
322	    --     so `Hom(κ, M) = 0`. Pass to `Ext^0` via `addEquiv₀`.
323	    --     For `1 ≤ i ≤ n`: the SES `0 → M →[x] M → M/xM → 0` (built via
324	    --     `IsSMulRegular.smulShortComplex_shortExact`) plus the fact that
325	    --     `[x]_*` is zero on `Ext^i(κ, M)` (because `x ∈ Ann κ` ⇒
326	    --     `x • 𝟙_κ = 0`, hence by `precomp_smul = smul_precomp` the
327	    --     R-action on `Ext^i(κ, M)` is annihilated by `x`) lets the
328	    --     LES connecting map `Ext^{i-1}(κ, M/xM) ↠ Ext^i(κ, M)` be
329	    --     surjective.  By IH applied to `M/xM` (we get `n ≤ depth (M/xM)`,
330	    --     so `Ext^j(κ, M/xM) = 0` for `j < n`) we conclude
331	    --     `Ext^i(κ, M) = 0` for `1 ≤ i ≤ n`.
332	    --
333	    -- (⇐) Assume `∀ i < n+1, ∀ e ∈ Ext^i(κ, M), e = 0`.
334	    --     Specialise at `i = 0` and use `Ext.addEquiv₀` to extract
335	    --     `Subsingleton (κ →ₗ[R] M)`.  Apply
336	    --     `IsSMulRegular.subsingleton_linearMap_iff` (Mathlib) with
337	    --     `N := ResidueField R` and `Module.annihilator R (ResidueField R) =
338	    --     maximalIdeal R` to obtain `x ∈ 𝔪` with `IsSMulRegular M x`.
339	    --     The SES + same "x annihilates Ext^*(κ, ·)" fact give
340	    --     `Ext^j(κ, M/xM) = 0` for `j < n` (via the LES at indices `j ≤ n-1`).
341	    --     `M/xM := QuotSMulTop x M` is `Nontrivial` by
342	    --     `nontrivial_quotSMulTop_of_mem_maximalIdeal` and `Module.Finite`
343	    --     as a quotient.  Apply `ih` on `M/xM` at index `n` to get a
344	    --     regular sequence `rs'` of length `n` in `𝔪` on `M/xM`.  Then
345	    --     `x :: rs'` is a regular sequence of length `n+1` in `𝔪` on `M`
346	    --     by `RingTheory.Sequence.isRegular_cons_iff`. This gives
347	    --     `(n+1 : ℕ∞) ≤ depth M` via `le_sSup` on the depth supremum.
348	    --
349	    -- iter-183: the helper `ext_smul_eq_zero_of_mem_annihilator` above closes
350	    -- the substantive piece (a) "`x ∈ Ann N` annihilates `Ext^i(N, M)`"; this
351	    -- is the algebraic fact under both directions of the iff. The remaining
352	    -- pieces are LES-of-Ext bookkeeping + supremum-extraction.
353	    --
354	    -- The remaining substantive Mathlib gaps for closing this step are:
355	    --   (b) Extracting `Module.annihilator R (ResidueField R) = maximalIdeal R`
356	    --       (a one-liner via `Ideal.annihilator_quotient`).
357	    --   (c) The "sSup gives a witness of length ≥ n+1" reasoning, which
358	    --       in `ℕ∞` requires handling the `⊤` case (Nakayama rules it out
359	    --       under `Nontrivial M`).
360	    --   (d) Lifting `IsSMulRegular`-cons via `isRegular_cons_iff`.
361	    --
362	    -- We open the backward direction's regular-element-extraction step
363	    -- below to expose the API path for the next iter; the forward
364	    -- direction's regular-sequence-extraction + LES chase is the larger
365	    -- body remaining as `sorry`.
366	    refine ⟨?_, ?_⟩
367	    · -- (⇒) Forward direction: `(n+1 : ℕ∞) ≤ depth M → ∀ i ≤ n, Ext^i(κ, M) = 0`.
368	      -- iter-184 Lane G: closed via Nakayama-driven `depth = sSup`
369	      -- extraction + cons-decomposition + LES chase using
370	      -- `ext_smul_eq_zero_of_mem_annihilator` + `covariant_sequence_exact₁`.
371	      intro _hdepth i _hi _e
372	      -- Step 1: unfold `depth M = sSup S_M` (Nakayama rules out `𝔪 • ⊤ = ⊤`).
373	      have hne_M : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) • (⊤ : Submodule R M) ≠ ⊤ :=
374	        Ne.symm (Submodule.top_ne_ideal_smul_of_le_jacobson_annihilator
375	          (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal_le_jacobson _))
376	      have hdepth_M_unfold :
377	          depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M
378	            = sSup { k : ℕ∞ | ∃ rs : List R, (rs.length : ℕ∞) = k ∧
379	              (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R)
380	              ∧ RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular M rs } := by
381	        rw [depth, if_neg hne_M]
382	      -- Step 2: extract a regular sequence rs of length > n in 𝔪 on M.
383	      have hlt : (n : ℕ∞) < depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M := by
384	        calc (n : ℕ∞)
385	            < ((n + 1 : ℕ) : ℕ∞) := by exact_mod_cast Nat.lt_succ_self n
386	          _ ≤ _ := _hdepth
387	      rw [hdepth_M_unfold] at hlt
388	      obtain ⟨k, ⟨rs, hrs_len, hrs_mem, hrs_reg⟩, hk_lt⟩ := lt_sSup_iff.mp hlt
389	      have hrs_len_gt : n < rs.length := by
390	        rw [← hrs_len] at hk_lt
391	        exact_mod_cast hk_lt
392	      -- Step 3: rs is non-empty (length > n ≥ 0); decompose rs = x :: rs_tail.
393	      rcases rs with _ | ⟨x, rs_tail⟩
394	      · -- impossible: empty list has length 0, not > n.
395	        simp at hrs_len_gt
396	      have hxMem : x ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R := hrs_mem x List.mem_cons_self
397	      have htail_mem : ∀ r ∈ rs_tail, r ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R := fun r hr =>
398	        hrs_mem r (List.mem_cons_of_mem _ hr)
399	      have hcons := (RingTheory.Sequence.isRegular_cons_iff M x rs_tail).mp hrs_reg
400	      have hxReg : IsSMulRegular M x := hcons.1
401	      have hrs_tail_reg : RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular (QuotSMulTop x M) rs_tail :=
402	        hcons.2
403	      have htail_len_ge : n ≤ rs_tail.length := by
404	        have h1 : n < (x :: rs_tail).length := hrs_len_gt
405	        simp [List.length_cons] at h1
406	        omega
407	      -- Step 4: `x ∈ Ann(κ)` via `Ideal.annihilator_quotient`.
408	      have hannih : Module.annihilator R (IsLocalRing.ResidueField R) =
409	          IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R := Ideal.annihilator_quotient
410	      have hxAnnih : x ∈ Module.annihilator R (IsLocalRing.ResidueField R) :=
411	        hannih ▸ hxMem
412	      -- Step 5: build MxM and show depth MxM ≥ n via the prefix rs_tail.take n.
413	      let MxM : Type u := QuotSMulTop x M
414	      haveI : Nontrivial MxM :=
415	        nontrivial_quotSMulTop_of_mem_maximalIdeal M hxMem
416	      haveI : _root_.Module.Finite R MxM := inferInstance
417	      have hne_MxM : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) • (⊤ : Submodule R MxM) ≠ ⊤ :=
418	        Ne.symm (Submodule.top_ne_ideal_smul_of_le_jacobson_annihilator
419	          (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal_le_jacobson _))
420	      let rs_n := rs_tail.take n
421	      have hrs_n_len : rs_n.length = n := by
422	        change (rs_tail.take n).length = n
423	        rw [List.length_take]
424	        omega
425	      have hrs_n_mem : ∀ r ∈ rs_n, r ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R := fun r hr =>
426	        htail_mem r (List.mem_of_mem_take hr)
427	      have hrs_n_reg : RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular MxM rs_n := by
428	        change RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular MxM (rs_tail.take n)
429	        have hsplit : rs_tail = rs_tail.take n ++ rs_tail.drop n :=
430	          (List.take_append_drop _ _).symm
431	        have hwr : RingTheory.Sequence.IsWeaklyRegular MxM rs_tail :=
432	          hrs_tail_reg.toIsWeaklyRegular
433	        rw [hsplit] at hwr
434	        have hwr_n : RingTheory.Sequence.IsWeaklyRegular MxM (rs_tail.take n) :=
435	          ((RingTheory.Sequence.isWeaklyRegular_append_iff MxM _ _).mp hwr).1
436	        exact (IsLocalRing.isRegular_iff_isWeaklyRegular_of_subset_maximalIdeal
437	          hrs_n_mem).mpr hwr_n
438	      have hdepth_MxM : (n : ℕ∞) ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) MxM := by
439	        rw [depth, if_neg hne_MxM]
440	        apply le_sSup
441	        refine ⟨rs_n, ?_, hrs_n_mem, hrs_n_reg⟩
442	        exact_mod_cast hrs_n_len

444	      have hMxM_vanish : ∀ j < n, ∀ e : Abelian.Ext.{u}
445	          (ModuleCat.of R (IsLocalRing.ResidueField R))
446	          (ModuleCat.of R MxM) j, e = 0 :=
447	        (ih (M := MxM)).mp hdepth_MxM

450	        ModuleCat.smulShortComplex (ModuleCat.of R M) x
451	      have hS : S.ShortExact := hxReg.smulShortComplex_shortExact
452	      -- `S.f = x • 𝟙_M` (definitional), hence `mk₀ S.f = x • mk₀ 𝟙`, hence

455	      have hSf_kill :
456	          _e.comp (CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.f) (add_zero i) = 0 := by
457	        have hcomp :
458	            _e.comp (CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.f) (add_zero i) = x • _e := by
459	          rw [hSf_eq_smul]
460	          exact (CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.smul_eq_comp_mk₀ _e x).symm
461	        rw [hcomp]
462	        exact ext_smul_eq_zero_of_mem_annihilator _e hxAnnih
463	      -- Split on i = 0 vs i ≥ 1.
464	      rcases Nat.eq_zero_or_pos i with hi0 | hi_pos
465	      · subst hi0
466	        -- Use mono S.f (since hxReg) + postcomp_mk₀_injective_of_mono.
467	        haveI hmono : CategoryTheory.Mono S.f := by
468	          rw [ModuleCat.mono_iff_injective]
469	          exact hxReg
470	        have hinj := CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.postcomp_mk₀_injective_of_mono
471	          (ModuleCat.of R (IsLocalRing.ResidueField R)) S.f
472	        apply hinj
473	        change _e.comp (CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.f) (add_zero 0)
474	          = (0 : Abelian.Ext.{u} (ModuleCat.of R (IsLocalRing.ResidueField R))
475	              (ModuleCat.of R M) 0).comp (CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.f)
476	              (add_zero 0)
477	        rw [CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.zero_comp]
478	        exact hSf_kill
479	      · -- i ≥ 1: write i = j + 1, then j < n and use covariant_sequence_exact₁.
480	        obtain ⟨j, rfl⟩ := Nat.exists_eq_succ_of_ne_zero
481	          (Nat.pos_iff_ne_zero.mp hi_pos)
482	        have hj : j < n := by omega
483	        obtain ⟨x₃, hx₃⟩ :=
484	          CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁

502	            (hext0 _)
503	        have hg : (ModuleCat.ofHom g : ModuleCat.of R _ ⟶ ModuleCat.of R M) = 0 :=
504	          (CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.mk₀_eq_zero_iff (ModuleCat.ofHom g)).mp
505	            (hext0 _)

507	        have hg' : g = 0 := ModuleCat.hom_ext_iff.mp hg
508	        rw [hf', hg']

524	      haveI : Nontrivial MxM :=

538	          (ModuleCat.of R MxM) j, e = 0 := by

540	        -- `e.comp hS.extClass rfl : Ext κ M (j+1) = 0` by `hext` at `j+1`.

622	
623	For a short exact sequence `0 → N' → N → N'' → 0` of nonzero finite modules
624	over a Noetherian local ring, the three modules' depths satisfy three
625	crosswise inequalities (Stacks tag 00LE), each a direct read-off of the

646	        (ModuleCat.of R (IsLocalRing.ResidueField R))

648	  have h' : ((i + 1 : ℕ) : ℕ∞) ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M := by
649	    have hcast : ((i + 1 : ℕ) : ℕ∞) = (i : ℕ∞) + 1 := by push_cast; ring

653	/-! ### Helper B (iter-182 Lane G, axiom-clean): `ℕ∞` tsub bridge
654	
655	If `(a : ℕ) ≤ d - 1` in `ℕ∞` and `1 ≤ a` (in `ℕ`), then

658	Case-split on `d = ⊤` (trivial) and `d = ↑n` (drop to `ℕ` arithmetic).

661	    {d : ℕ∞} {a : ℕ} (ha : 1 ≤ a) (h : (a : ℕ∞) ≤ d - 1) :

664	  · simp [hd]
665	  · obtain ⟨n, rfl⟩ := WithTop.ne_top_iff_exists.mp hd

668	      refine h.trans (le_of_eq ?_)

670	      · rfl
671	      · push_cast; rfl
672	    have han : a ≤ n - 1 := by exact_mod_cast h₂
673	    have hle : a + 1 ≤ n := by omega
674	    exact Nat.cast_le.mpr hle
